evidence_text: SWE-bench contains 2,294 software engineering task instances drawn from real GitHub issues and corresponding pull requests across 12 popular Python repositories.
source_excerpt: "We introduce SWE-bench, an evaluation framework consisting of 2,294 software engineering problems drawn from real GitHub issues and corresponding pull requests across 12 popular Python repositories."
note: This is a benchmark composition claim from the paper’s abstract and benchmark description.
caveat: This is a benchmark composition claim from the paper’s abstract and benchmark description.

evidence_text: SWE-bench task construction uses a 3-stage pipeline: repo selection and PR scraping, attribute-based filtering, and execution-based filtering.
source_excerpt: "To find high-quality task instances at scale, we use a 3-stage pipeline as follows." The stages are then listed as "Stage I: Repo selection and data scraping", "Stage II: Attribute-based filtering", and "Stage III: Execution-based filtering."
note: The paper presents this as the core benchmark-construction method.
caveat: The paper presents this as the core benchmark-construction method.

evidence_text: The authors initially collected about 90,000 pull requests from 12 popular open-source Python repositories before filtering.
source_excerpt: "We start by collecting pull requests (PRs) from 12 popular open-source Python repositories on GitHub, producing about ∼90,000 PRs in total."
note: This describes the pre-filtering scale of the raw source data.
caveat: This describes the pre-filtering scale of the raw source data.

evidence_text: Candidate tasks were limited to merged pull requests that resolve a GitHub issue, modify test files, include at least one fail-to-pass test, and do not produce installation or runtime errors.
source_excerpt: "Stage II: Attribute-based filtering. We create candidate tasks by selecting the merged PRs that (1) resolve a GitHub issue and (2) make changes to the test files of the repository." "We filter out task instances without at least one test where its status changes from a fail to pass... We also filter out instances that result in installation or runtime errors."
note: This combines the explicit filtering criteria across stages II and III.
caveat: This combines the explicit filtering criteria across stages II and III.

evidence_text: The filtering pipeline reduced roughly 90,000 pull requests to 2,294 task instances.
source_excerpt: "Through these stages of filtering, the original 90,000 PRs are filtered down to the 2,294 task instances which comprise SWE-bench."
note: This states the scale reduction from raw PRs to final benchmark tasks.
caveat: This states the scale reduction from raw PRs to final benchmark tasks.

evidence_text: A model solution is counted as successful only if the generated patch applies successfully and all associated unit and system tests pass.
source_excerpt: "If the patch applies successfully and all of these tests pass we consider the proposed solution to have successfully resolved the issue. The metric for our benchmark is the percentage of task instances that are resolved."
note: This defines the benchmark success criterion and headline metric.
caveat: This defines the benchmark success criterion and headline metric.

evidence_text: SWE-bench issue descriptions average 195 words, non-test codebases average 3,010 files and 438K lines, and reference solutions average edits to 1.7 files, 3.0 functions, and 32.8 lines.
source_excerpt: "Issue descriptions are typically long and detailed (195 words on average), and codebases regularly contain many thousands of files." Table 1 reports: "Issue Text Length (Words) 195.1", "Codebase # Files (non-test) 3,010", "# Lines (non-test) 438K", and "Gold Patch # Lines edited 32.8", "# Files edited 1.7", "# Func. edited 3".
note: These are summary statistics for benchmark instance size and edit scope.
caveat: These are summary statistics for benchmark instance size and edit scope.

evidence_text: Each task instance has at least one fail-to-pass test, 40% of instances have at least two fail-to-pass tests, and the median instance runs 51 additional tests to check preserved functionality.
source_excerpt: "For each task instance, there is at least one fail-to-pass test... and 40% of instances have at least two fail-to-pass tests." "In addition, a median of 51 additional tests run to check whether prior functionality is properly maintained."
note: These statistics describe the benchmark’s test coverage and preservation checks.
caveat: These statistics describe the benchmark’s test coverage and preservation checks.

evidence_text: Under BM25 retrieval on the full SWE-bench benchmark, Claude 2 resolved 1.97% of tasks, GPT-4-turbo 1.31%, ChatGPT-3.5 0.17%, SWE-Llama 7b 0.70%, and SWE-Llama 13b 0.70%.
source_excerpt: Table 5 reports for SWE-bench: "Claude 2 1.97", "ChatGPT-3.5 0.17", "GPT-4-turbo 1.31", "SWE-Llama 7b 0.70", and "SWE-Llama 13b 0.70" under "% Resolved."
note: The abstract rounds Claude 2’s result to 1.96%; Table 5 reports 1.97%.
caveat: The abstract rounds Claude 2’s result to 1.96%; Table 5 reports 1.97%.

evidence_text: The paper states that current evaluated models struggle broadly on SWE-bench, with the best-performing model solving only about 2% of issues under the main BM25 setting.
source_excerpt: "Across the board, models struggle significantly to resolve issues. The best performing model, Claude 2, is only able to resolve 1.96% of the issues."
note: This is a summary interpretation attached to reported benchmark results; the exact percentage varies slightly between abstract text and Table 5 (1.96% vs. 1.97%).
caveat: This is a summary interpretation attached to reported benchmark results; the exact percentage varies slightly between abstract text and Table 5 (1.96% vs. 1.97%).

evidence_text: With a 27,000-token context limit, BM25 retrieved a superset of oracle files in about 40% of instances, but in nearly half of instances it retrieved none of the oracle files.
citation: Section 4.1 BM25 recall and retrieval coverage
source_excerpt: "We observe that in approximately 40% of instances, BM25 retrieves a superset of the oracle files for the 27,000-token context limit. However, in almost half of the instances with the 27,000-token limit, it retrieves none of the files from the 'oracle' context."
note: This evidence shows retrieval quality is uneven and introduces a direct caveat on context selection.
caveat: This evidence shows retrieval quality is uneven and introduces a direct caveat on context selection.

evidence_text: Claude 2 resolved 4.8% of issues with oracle retrieval and 5.9% with oracle-collapsed retrieval, both higher than its roughly 2% result under BM25 retrieval.
source_excerpt: "There, Claude 2 is able to resolve 4.8% of issues using the 'oracle' retriever." Later: "we see increases in performance... Claude 2 from 4.8% to 5.9%." Table 6 lists Claude 2 in the "Oracle"-collapsed setting with "Resolved 5.93".
note: These results indicate performance changes materially with more targeted context, but the oracle settings are less realistic than the main retrieval setup.
caveat: These results indicate performance changes materially with more targeted context, but the oracle settings are less realistic than the main retrieval setup.

evidence_text: Increasing context size improved BM25 recall relative to oracle files but reduced model performance, and the authors report that models often struggled to localize the relevant code within larger contexts.
source_excerpt: "We see that as total context length increases, Claude 2's performance drops considerably; behavior that is also observed in other models." "Even when increasing the maximum context size for BM25 would increase recall with respect to the oracle files, performance drops... as models are simply ineffective at localizing problematic code."
note: This is mechanism-relevant because it identifies a proposed failure mode, but it is bounded to the reported evaluation setting and author interpretation.
caveat: This is mechanism-relevant because it identifies a proposed failure mode, but it is bounded to the reported evaluation setting and author interpretation.

evidence_text: The SWE-Llama training set was built from 19,000 issue-PR pairs from 37 additional Python repositories, and LoRA fine-tuning excluded training sequences longer than 30,000 tokens, reducing the effective training corpus to 10,000 instances.
source_excerpt: "We introduce SWE-bench, an evaluation framework consisting of 2,294 software engineering problems drawn from real GitHub issues and corresponding pull requests across 12 popular Python repositories."
note: This is a benchmark composition claim from the paper’s abstract and benchmark description.
caveat: This is a benchmark composition claim from the paper’s abstract and benchmark description.
